title: Cullen skink
subtitle: Scottish smoked haddock and potato soup.
excerpt: Smoked haddock poached gently in milk, then flaked into a soup of softened leek, onion, and floury potato. Thick, smoky, the colour of clouds. The signature dish of the Moray Firth fishing town that gave it its name.
sourceNotes: F. Marian McNeill, The Scots Kitchen (1929), records the dish as it has been made in the village of Cullen on the Moray Firth. The word "skink" is Scots for a soup-stew, especially one made with shins of beef; the fish version is the local specialty.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 2 months; the texture softens slightly on reheat. Defrost overnight, reheat gently without boiling.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better on day two. Reheat gently; don't boil or the fish will toughen.

glossaryTerms.definition: A Scots word for a soup made by long simmering, originally with shins of beef. By the time of F. Marian McNeill's Scots Kitchen (1929), the smoked-haddock version from Cullen had become the dominant meaning of the word in cookery.

content.text: A piece of smoked haddock, ideally Finnan haddie or Arbroath smoke, undyed and properly cured, is poached in milk and water for 8 minutes. The fish comes out, the milk is reserved, and the soup base is built in the same pan: butter, finely chopped leek and onion, softened until translucent. Floury potato goes in, cut into 1 cm cubes. The reserved milk is poured over and the soup simmers until the potato is tender. Then the fish is flaked back in.
The traditional Cullen version is thickened by mashing some of the cooked potato against the side of the pan. No flour, no cream — the soup gets its body from potato starch and the milk.

items.prepNote: white and pale green only, finely sliced
King Edward or other floury type, peeled and cut into 1 cm cubes
to taste; smoked haddock is salty

content.text: A heavy 3 litre saucepan wide enough to take the haddock flat for poaching.

Lay {{smoked-haddock}} of haddock in the saucepan, skin down. Pour over {{whole-milk}} of milk and {{water}} of water. Drop in {{bay-leaves}} of bay leaves and {{black-pepper}} of peppercorns. Bring slowly to a bare simmer over low heat. Cook 8 minutes; bubbles should just break the surface. Lift the fish out onto a plate with a fish slice. Strain the poaching liquid through a fine sieve and reserve.

Wipe the pan with kitchen paper. Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter over medium heat. Add {{leek}} of sliced leek and {{onion}} of chopped onion with a pinch of salt. Cook 10 minutes, stirring often, until soft and translucent without colour.
Tip in {{potato}} of cubed potato. Pour over the reserved poaching milk. Bring to a gentle simmer. Cook 15 minutes, partially covered, until the potato is tender to a knife.
Mash a generous third of the potato against the side of the pan with a wooden spoon. The released starch will thicken the soup.

Skin the haddock and flake into large chunks, checking for pin bones. Stir gently through the soup. Pour in the optional double cream. Heat through for 2 minutes; don't let it boil. Taste and add {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt if needed. Rest off the heat 5 minutes. Ladle into deep bowls and scatter with {{parsley-flat}} of chopped parsley.

items.fix: Keep the heat low — bubbles just breaking the surface. Eight minutes is enough for a 400 g piece of haddock.

items.cause: Potato not mashed against the pan.
items.fix: Mash at least a third of the cooked potato against the side of the pan to release starch. Floury potatoes (King Edward, Maris Piper) thicken the soup; waxy ones don't.

items.cause: Boiled after the fish went in.
items.fix: Keep the heat low at the final stage — fish, cream, and salty milk all benefit from a gentle warm-through, not a boil.
Don't add salt until the very end. Smoked haddock carries a heavy salt cure already; taste before adding more.

content.text: Cullen skink belongs to the fishing villages of the Moray Firth, where smoked haddock has been cured for centuries. F. Marian McNeill wrote the dish into print in 1929. It pairs with a slice of crusty bread and a cold winter afternoon. A small dram of Speyside whisky on the side wouldn't be out of place.
